replacement_html:
Robustness under refraction

The dome publishes the Sun altitude as two values: an observed value (5,733 km, attributed to aetheric refraction) and a physical value (under 4,200 km, the firmament limit). The geometric calculation above uses observed altitudes because the visible eclipse follows light paths, not straight lines from an unobserved physical Sun. Repeating the calculation with physical altitudes — hSun ≤ 4,200 km, hMoon scaled in proportion to ~1,860 km — yields a totality circle ~0.97 km in diameter, sharpening the mismatch rather than softening it. The result is robust to alternative refractive scalings: even leaving hMoon at 2,534 km only widens totality to ~1.85 km, still ~1/160 of the observed 294-km path. To recover that path through refraction, the dome must publish a ray-tracing or refractive-shadow mechanism that (a) is not derivable from the standard occultation formula AND (b) reproduces the observed path width and station-by-station coverage values. None is published in formula_runtime.json; the dome's own observed/physical altitude distinction therefore narrows the trilemma rather than offering a fourth way out.

math_verification.method: Independent Python re-derivation matching the curmudgeon's c1 reproduction methodology
math_verification.rounding_note: Paragraph cites ~0.97 km diameter (rounded from 0.964) and ~1.85 km (asymmetric upper bound). Curmudgeon c1 cited ~0.9 km — same calculation with looser rounding. EXP-262 tracker notes mistakenly read ~0.58 km, which does not reproduce under any reasonable refractive scaling (no sensitivity case yields <0.4 km radius without making the umbra cone fail to reach the disc). Used the math-verified ~0.97 km figure in the prose.
math_verification.operator_check: Re-derived from first principles in Python; results match the curmudgeon's c1 review independently.

holes_addressed: H1 (curmudgeon EXP-254 c1, severity moderate): preempts dome's observed-vs-physical Sun altitude distinction and the implied aetheric-refraction defensive opening — converts a latent rebuttal vector into visible robustness.
new_evidence_added: Repeated geometric derivation under physical altitudes — totality diameter ~0.97 km — sharpens, does not soften, the mismatch.
Sensitivity analysis under asymmetric scaling (h_Sun=4200, h_Moon=2534) — totality only widens to ~1.85 km, still ~1/160 of observed 294 km.

Explicit demand on the dome: any refractive-shadow rescue must (a) be non-derivable from standard occultation formula, AND (b) reproduce both observed path width AND station-by-station coverage values.
Direct citation that formula_runtime.json publishes no such mechanism — same artifact already cited in EXP-254 §4.2.7.

anticipated_objections.objection: Proportional scaling of h_Moon is your assumption, not the dome's. The dome only publishes a physical altitude for the Sun.
anticipated_objections.response: The paragraph addresses this explicitly: even with h_Moon held at the published 2,534 km and only the Sun dropped to 4,200 km, totality only widens to ~1.85 km — still ~1/160 of the observed 294-km path. Both proportional and asymmetric scalings yield catastrophic mismatch.

anticipated_objections.objection: Refraction can produce arbitrary path widths if the refractive index n(r) is permitted to vary freely.
anticipated_objections.response: The paragraph imposes a falsifiable bar: any refractive mechanism the dome publishes must (a) not be derivable from standard occultation, AND (b) reproduce path width and station-by-station coverage. n(r) bent to fit any observation is, by construction, parasitic on the very physics the dome rejects (option 2 of the trilemma).

anticipated_objections.objection: 0.97 km vs 0.9 km is a discrepancy with the curmudgeon's c1 figure.
anticipated_objections.response: Same calculation; rounding only. Curmudgeon's c1 used h_Moon=1860 (rounded); the paragraph uses h_Moon=2534×(4200/5733)=1856.4 (more precise), yielding 0.964 km diameter, cited as ~0.97 km. The two figures agree to within rounding precision.

anticipated_objections.objection: EXP-262 tracker notes claimed ~0.58 km. Why did you cite ~0.97 km?
anticipated_objections.response: The 0.58 km figure does not reproduce under any reasonable refractive scaling I tested. Going below ~0.8 km diameter requires either h_Moon falling below the umbra-cone-reach threshold (no totality at all — strengthening the argument differently) or an unphysical mismatch between h_Sun and h_Moon. Used the math-verified figure that the curmudgeon's c1 reproduction also derived (~0.9 km). Documented the discrepancy and the rederivation in math_verification.rounding_note for traceability.

kernel_of_truth: The observed-vs-physical altitude distinction is not arbitrary obscurantism on the dome's part — it reflects a real conceptual problem (how to reconcile telescopically-measured apparent positions with putative actual positions on a flat disc), and refractive-index models in classical optics genuinely can produce surprising apparent-position effects. The dome is correct that observed altitudes need not equal physical altitudes; ophthalmologists, atmospheric scientists, and astronomers all routinely distinguish the two.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: The kernel grants conceptual legitimacy to the distinction but the paragraph's math-verified result is that proportional refractive scaling makes the geometric mismatch WORSE, and even asymmetric scaling cannot widen totality past 1.85 km — still 159x narrower than observed. Refraction is a real phenomenon; the question is whether the dome can publish an n(r) ray-tracing mechanism that reproduces both observed path width AND station-by-station coverage values without smuggling in globe geometry. The kernel does not answer that question; until the mechanism is published, the trilemma stands.
other_side_of_the_argument: The strongest defender pushback against this paragraph: 'You haven't actually tested a refractive ray-tracing model, only a re-derivation under straight-line geometry with different altitudes. A genuine n(r) ray-tracing solution might bend light from a 4,200-km-high Sun in such a way that the apparent shadow on the disc covers a 294-km path despite the local geometry suggesting otherwise.' This is a fair pushback and the paragraph addresses it head-on — by demanding the dome publish such a model. The paragraph deliberately does not pre-emptively rule out the existence of such a mechanism (we cannot prove a negative); instead, it states the falsifiable bar the dome must clear. If a genuine n(r) ray-tracing model is published that meets bars (a) and (b), the paragraph (and the §4.2.7 trilemma's option 1 escape) is the place to revisit. The current state — repeated invocation of 'aetheric refraction' as a placeholder without a published ray-tracing model — is a category error analogous to the FSF tan(latitude) tautology in §4.2.4: a label substituted for a derivation. Per the user's standing 'consider the other side' directive, this risk is acknowledged, the falsifiable bar is set high but achievable, and the revisit clause is preserved.

out_of_scope_explicit: This expansion does not address curmudgeon EXP-254 holes H2-H7 (separate EXPs or pre-integration fold for those).
This expansion does not modify EXP-254's TLDR — the trilemma argument is unchanged; only its robustness is made visible.
This expansion does not propose new SVG figures.
build_impact: Minimal. Additive HTML in §4.2.7 between two existing <h3> subsections. No new files, no new asset references, no test changes. Word count delta +165; total §4.2.7 replacement word count rises from 1754 → 1919.
notes_for_decider: Two sequencing options. Pre-integration fold (preferred): edit EXP-254's replacement_html in place before EXP-254 integrates, single integration step covers both. Post-integration patch (fallback): apply as a separate substring-replacement in part4.html after EXP-254 lands. Curmudgeon's c1 explicitly recommended pre-integration fold (see EXP-254-proposal.json suggested_sequencing). Either works; pre-integration is cleaner and avoids a re-build between fixes.
